title: Disappointing & Boring
text: Only decided to watch the movie because the main actor used to have a pretty good role in the TV series Narcos not that long ago. But the storyline and directing of this movie is very disappointing. I watched the first 30 minutes and just couldn't continue anymore because I'm confused on what the story is about and where the movie is going. Lots of random pieces without any cohesiveness or attraction especially to someone who's not familiar with that era or that area of the world. Pretty boring to say the least.
author: HighlanderABC

title: A Film of Exceptional Form and Unresolved Meaning
text: The Secret Agent is a film that immediately commands attention through its formal mastery. From the very first frames it convincingly recreates the atmosphere of 1970s Brazil with remarkable precision. The production design and costume work feel historically authentic rather than decorative and the cinematography reinforces this illusion with disciplined framing and a classical visual language that rarely draws attention to itself yet remains consistently striking.Several sequences stand out as pure cinema. The chase sequence is tightly constructed and rhythmically precise creating real tension without relying on excessive editing. The framing and mise en scène throughout the film show a director fully in control of the frame and confident in visual storytelling. In purely formal terms the film is often excellent.Almost every actor delivers a believable portrayal, responding to the tense political and social environment with subtlety and restraint. The interactions feel lived-in and authentic, creating a tangible sense of reality within the stylized historical setting. That said, Wagner Moura, while consistently present and controlled in his performance, does not reach the same level of nuance as some of best performances we witnessed this year as an actor in leading role . His portrayal is solid and reliable but lacks the subtle shifts and emotional depth that might have elevated the film's most intimate moments.The character of Dona Sebastiana is one of the film's strongest achievements. She feels alive layered and grounded in social reality. Unlike many other figures in the film she carries a clear sense of personal history and presence and every scene involving her feels purposeful and emotionally anchored.However where the film begins to struggle is in its narrative structure. The story repeatedly withholds crucial information without providing a clear dramatic or thematic justification. Key power dynamics remain vague. Motivations are implied rather than established and major events unfold without sufficient causal clarity. This does not create productive ambiguity but instead results in narrative gaps that weaken emotional engagement.The film's political dimension is visually persuasive but dramatically underdeveloped. Power is shown as omnipresent yet rarely explained. Characters with immense influence appear and act without the film clearly establishing who they are or how their authority functions. As a result political tension exists more as atmosphere than as narrative force.The time jumps and fast forward sections further complicate the experience. While they suggest a thematic contrast between past repression and a calmer present the execution is confusing. The decision to cast the same actor in multiple generational roles without meaningful physical or psychological differentiation undermines any intended statement about historical change. Rather than clarity the result is interpretive noise.The ending gesture in which Marcelo turns rival groups against each other is conceptually intriguing and visually effective. It suggests an understanding of how power fragments responsibility and manufactures internal conflict. Yet even this idea is left underexplored and lacks sufficient narrative groundwork to fully resonate.Ultimately The Secret Agent feels like a film that prioritizes form mood and historical texture over narrative resolution. It succeeds powerfully as an aesthetic and atmospheric experience but falls short as a coherent political drama. Its ambiguities feel less like deliberate artistic choices and more like unresolved writing decisions.This is a film to admire and to question often at the same time.
author: mehdimellatdoust

title: A Listening Film in an Age of Noise
text: O Agente Secreto, directed by Kleber Mendonça Filho, arrives less as a thriller than as a patient act of excavation. A work that resists spectacle, preferring instead the slow pressure of atmosphere, memory, and political residue. This is a cinema of vigilance-one that watches as much as it withholds.The screenplay's apparent minimalism is deceptive. What is left unsaid carries the weight of censorship, fear, and complicity. Dialogue becomes an echo chamber where language is constantly surveilled, never fully free.Accordingly, the narrative does not advance through twists or revelations, but through accretion. Information gathers like dust, like layers of sediment, until the viewer senses the weight of what has accumulated. The screenplay's apparent minimalism is therefore deceptive. What initially seems sparse or restrained gradually reveals itself as dense with implication. Dialogue often skirts around its own subject, pauses linger uncomfortably, and silence assumes a semantic weight equal to speech. Meaning is never handed over; it must be inferred, assembled, and sometimes suspected rather than known.At the center of this carefully modulated world stands Wagner Moura, whose performance has been widely singled out for its internal gravitas. A man under continuous negotiation with his surroundings. He embodies a subject shaped less by decisive action than by the necessity of caution, a man whose interior life has been disciplined by fear and history.The film refuses to treat the past as something concluded or safely archived. Instead, it seeps into the present, contaminating gestures, spaces, and relationships. History is not recalled; it persists. In this sense, the film becomes a meditation on how systems of control colonize subjectivity, shaping not only behavior but perception itself.The strength of this meditation is amplified by an ensemble cast that operates with remarkable cohesion. This is a clear example of how a well-structured and attuned group of actors can transform a film, creating a social texture in which no character feels incidental. Each presence contributes to a collective atmosphere of unease, reinforcing the idea that control is systemic rather than isolated.Embedded within this larger structure are the film's micro-narratives - small, seemingly peripheral stories that open sudden, abyssal depths: the war hero, the bizarre attack of the "hairy leg", the abandoned corpse, the divorced dentist, the disappeared former resident... These microcontos function as narrative fractures, brief windows into lives marked by absurdity, violence, or quiet despair. They do not advance the plot, yet they deepen the world, suggesting that behind every surface lies another story, equally unresolved and equally haunted.Equally striking is the film's metanarrative dimension, in which cinema itself becomes a living component of the story. A significant portion of the narrative unfolds inside a movie theater, literally situating the characters - and by extension, the audience - within a space of representation. In the midst of chaos, cinema appears as a refuge, but also as an ironic mirror: the horror film on screen is a caricature of the far more insidious terror of reality. The title makes this relationship explicit. The protagonist emerges as a kaleidoscopic variation of John Drake (Danger Man), a reminder that art distorts, refracts, and parodies life.In the end, O Agente Secreto leaves the viewer suspended in a state of unresolved awareness. It is not a film that seeks to explain or absolve, but one that insists on attention, on patience, and on the uncomfortable recognition that the past is never truly past. It watches us as we watch it, and in that mutual vigilance, it reveals the quiet, enduring violence of systems that never fully disappear.
author: luis-boaventura22

title: My review got deleted! Why?
text: I'm Brazilian, politically on the left and a fan of Wagner Moura, but this movie is not good. Normally, I'm extremely tolerant when it comes to movies. I even like to watch bad movies, such as "Manos: The Hands of Fate", because they are so bad that they are good. "O Agente Secreto", however, is just plain bad. It's so bad that I even left the cinema in the middle of the film (something I almost never do). This movie is not only slow and waaaaay overlong, it also doesn't know what kind of movie it wants to be. The story is all over the place, with loads of blablabla, characters and situations that cutting them out would change nothing in the story, which lacks suspense anyway. Movies like this win prizes not because they are well-made, but because they are "exotic" (Europeans love that) and because of their political message. I wasted my time, but at least I didn't waste my money. It was a free ticket.PS: The movie's title is total nonsense.
author: Freethinker_Atheist

title: Leggless and Pointless for me
text:
My Review- The Secret Agent
In Cinemas now
My Rating 5/10It always puzzles me as to why foreign language movies are eligible for a double dip at the Oscar's to possibly win Best Movie in a Foreign Language as well as Best Movie of the year? So I did some research and the short answer was that the Academy's top prize has never had a language restriction. If a film meets the general eligibility it can compete for Best Picture regardless of language or country of origin.For decades, international films were effectively siloed or ignored so the Academy decided that allowing TV crossover of categories prevented the ghettoisation of non-English cinema signalling that world cinema is not "separate but equal" plus acknowledging that a masterpiece doesn't become "lesser" because it has subtitles.My question to the Academy is by including two foreign language movies as they have this year in the Best Film as well as Best foreign language creates a disadvantage to two of the best English language movies eg -Nuremberg or The Choral .Oh well , let's talk about The Secret Agent the highly praised Brazilian movie that's nominated for 4 Oscars this year including Best Picture, Best International Feature Movie , Best Actor for its star Wagner Mourer and Best Casting.If I was voting I wouldn't vote for The Secret Agent in any of those categories because I found it to long at 161 minutes, to disjointed and while realising the script may be better than the subtitles suggest not very interesting.Set in 1977 Brazil Wagner Mourer is a good choice for the role of Marcelo a technology expert who becomes a fugitive on the run.Hoping to reunite with his son, he travels to Recife during Carnival but soon realizes that the city is not the safe haven he was expecting.It may well have got lost in translation however it's completion from Norway Sentimental Value for me is a far superior movie in every way because I could identify with every character and every scene interested me .If anyone can explain the bizarre scene in this movie of a severed leg that was found in a sharks belly suddenly coming to life while kicking gay men in a park I'd appreciate finding an answer?The continual toing and froing back and forth made the timeline of the movie confusing for me and the conclusion after so many unnecessary scenes seemed rushed and unexplained.I've seen some wonderful Brazilian movies like the 1998 Central Station and the stunning I'm Still Here 2025 but I can't add The Secret Agent to my list.
author: tm-sheehan

title: An exceptional movie lost in translation
text: Even though I am Portuguese and familiar with Brazilian Portuguese, I watched *Agente Secreto* with English subtitles. The translation is very poor. For viewers who do not know Brazilian Portuguese idiomatic expressions, it will be very difficult to understand many of the dialogues, how rich they are, and to grasp the 1970s Brazilian atmosphere, which is essential to understanding the plot and the characters. Wagner Moura is an exceptional actor, and his performance in this film is absolutely memorable. I fear that many viewers will never be able to appreciate the enormous cinematic work that this film represents and Kleber Mendonça Filho's talent because of the difficulty in relating to the events and Brazilian reality of the 1970s, in addition to the language barrier.
author: jjntavares

title: A Misleading Title, Powered by Misleading Hype
text: If I could give this movie one star, I would (I can, but out of respect for Moura, I won't).I really wanted to like this film. I was extremely hyped before watching it, and I watched it attentively, yet it kept disappointing me minute after minute. The movie's potentially amazing story failed to deliver its quality for several reasons.First, the pacing is highly problematic. During the first half of the film, you're barely given any hints about what the story is actually about, and you're introduced to a variety of characters who add nothing to the plot or the main character's development. Then the film finally gets to the core of what happened, but even there you're given only incoherent fragments, with some parts making no sense whatsoever.And the finale completely ruined it for me. After so much back-and-forth, the main character's ending is one of the most underwhelming I've ever seen. I honestly felt betrayed and angry.The film is also far too long. It should have been at least an hour shorter and features about five characters more than necessary.All in all, one of the biggest disappointments of last/this year.
author: andreadjokickv

title: underwhelming and overrated
text: Heard great things about this movie but it's just a big mess. A visual spectacle that falls short in its story telling. Act 1 starts off with a compelling story but after two more acts you will still have the same questions you started with. Don't recommend putting almost 3 hours of your life onto this movie.
author: p-28597

title: Not for everyone - complicated, cagy, and dense. But worth it
text: I saw "The Secret Agent" on 01/28/2026 at the theaters. I went because I was curious about this movie's Oscar recognition: it was nominated for Best Picture and Best Actor ... altogether, it's a complicated, cagy, and dense work. But after making it to the end and reflecting on what I saw - I feel it was worth it.I'll begin with what I like -- the movie, on a technical level, is nice to look at. The color scheme is welcoming; I felt as if I could feel the 1970s Brazilian sunset through the screen. Wagner Moura's character drives a beautiful yellow car in the movie.I have no knowledge about Brazilian history, but the movie captures the rampant crime, corruption, and seediness of this country's era. The opening scene in the gas station might be the best scene in the movie. The end of the movie is not what I expected, but I found it moving and thoughtful; the message of the movie is worth understanding.All that said -the movie is like a labyrinth. There are all sorts of narrative shifts, digressions, and vagueness. The following often happened: the movie would show a sequence, I would get confused about something, a scene much later would occur to offer new details to clarify, and then I would have to recollect on the earlier scene to apply the new information to make that confusing earlier scene make sense ... does it sound hard to understand? It kinda is .... In short, the movie seemingly expects the viewer to unpack a lot of scenes long after those scenes have occurred in the 2 hr + 40 minute runtime. Some details go totally unexplained in the end.The movie is too long. I could've done without some scenes, like a bizarre leg subplot as well as a sequence involving a World War 2 veteran ... Some character development was necessary. The movie should've better established the father/son connection as well as the main character's trauma. About the latter, all the movie gives is one brief scene where he has a nightmare at night. The effect is that I felt as if I couldn't grasp the character's predicament ... on a minor note, there are some bad prosthetics in a certain shooting sequenceWagner Moura is serviceable as the lead, but I did not see Oscar-worthy. Most of the time, he merely conveys stoicism or blankness. He's not mind-blowingly good as some people say, and there are multiple actors whom I would've nominated for the Oscar instead6.5/10.
author: redban02

title: A compelling and masterfully crafted cinematic achievement.
text: Kleber Mendonça Filho's "O Agente Segreto" is a brilliant, genre-defying tour de force. It masterfully weaves sharp political critique with deeply human drama, creating a film that is both intellectually provocative and profoundly moving. The direction is confident and visionary, the screenplay is razor-sharp, and the performances are uniformly outstanding. It is a powerful, essential work that showcases the vitality and world-class artistry of Brazilian cinema. Fully deserving of its place among the Oscar contenders. He deserves to win.
author: Gleydson-3

title: Generic Drama but Great world.
text: The Secret Agent keeps throwing elements into a mix of emotional and political drama, but they don't stick together. The generic character arcs are left hanging as they are without a satisfactory closure. However, the world it's trying to build and sell works great, it is so bold that they don't even show us the years and just make the statement with its world's context for us to pick up.
author: yothisisyo

title: A Beautiful Nothingness
text: Impressive acting for the protagonist in his first film, apparently, beautiful colors, great locations, great production, and reconstruction of the 70's vibes, every single scene was made expertly.After a beautiful introduction to his life, what's up with this story? There are too many holes and mistakes; why lose half of the movie's time with the recording of the tape? I lost all connection with the movie at the halfway mark. I was almost shocked by how the movie started to go downhill. Such a waste of acting skills ruined by a story that still makes no sense to me. Was this taken from a book? If so, why did nobody think that was a boring story, and do something more original, even if the story is true, is a big pile of nothing?So much hard work for something that could be made for an episode of some thriller Series done in 25 minutes. People giving 10/10 are delusional. If this had a great story could have been a Brazilian masterpiece of cinematography. Let's hope the next film the director gets some compelling story to work with.
author: Gl0bal_Warning

title: overrated
text: The movie is just overrated and boring. No big plot twist. It seems like it was created to serve a political agenda. The story is actually nice, but i could be developed better. author: babydu-85006

title: Hyped movie
text: The film stands out primarily for its impeccable setting and carefully composed cinematography. The re-creation of 1977 Recife offers a convincing immersion into the dense, oppressive, and politically tense atmosphere of the dictatorship, with richly detailed environments and visual work that reinforces the constant sense of surveillance and paranoia. It's a film that immediately captivates through its aesthetic precision and striking imagery.The supporting cast is another strong point: secondary actors and actresses deliver intense performances, full of nuance and presence. However, the strength of these interpretations contrasts with the limited development the script grants them. There are visibly promising characters who end up underexplored, serving more as fragments of a larger universe than as fully realized figures within the narrative.The film's biggest stumble lies in its uneven script, which manages to build an initial sense of danger but gradually dismantles it as the story progresses and loses focus. This is compounded by a lead performance that, while competent, feels predictable: Wagner Moura, despite his undeniable charisma, repeats mannerisms already familiar from his previous roles. The final result is a technically brilliant film with powerful moments, but one that leaves the impression of not fully achieving the potential it seemed to promise.
author: thalisson-ovelar-243-406515

title: A great thematically rich timepiece
text: Brazil is in the spotlight right now with two movies. First, it was I'M STILL HERE. Now, it's the Golden Globe-winning historical neo-noir political thriller THE SECRET AGENT. While I'M STILL HERE took place in the South-East region of Rio, this one takes place in the North-East region of Recife. Both movies feature the nightmarish military dictatorship of the 1970's, but in different ways. Corruption, disregard for truth, and indifference to suffering are common in this time and place. It shows a dark piece of Brazilian history. The opening scene is a masterclass of suspense, setting the tone for what we're about to watch. It's a story about Marcelo (Wagner Moura), a man who's trying to lay low. I can't really say too much more about him, but he ends up living with people like him. I really enjoyed Dona Sebastiana, played by Tânia Maria. She takes him in and deserves her own movie. I enjoyed this experience, but not as much as a lot of movie critics. I just think maybe some things went above my head a bit. It has intentional ambiguity, perhaps too much for me, especially because I was busy reading subtitles. There's a subplot involving a human leg that's found inside a shark's mouth. There's a theme of how a government can steer the narrative in whatever way that most benefits them. The characters are fully fleshed out. Moura is a movie star, already winning a Golden Globe for this performance. This film is multifaceted. It's a love letter to cinema and the preservation of it. The preservation of culture itself. There was one storytelling choice I didn't really get, but I believe I'm getting close to. Overall, THE SECRET AGENT is an unconventional slow burn thriller that takes its time getting the audience used to its dangerous environment and time period. It's thematically rich and has amazing performances. It has been nominated for four Academy Awards: Best International Film, Best Actor for Moura, Best Casting, and Best Picture. This film is very important to Brazil and its people, as it tries to heal the country's fractured memory of a horrible dictatorship. We don't want history repeating itself, right?
author: stevencsmovies

title: PUTS CERTAIN OSCAR/BAFTA NOMINATED MOVIES TO SHAME
text: Just finished watching this extraordinary multi faceted Brazilan / Portuguese language movie which explores similar themes as the outrageously over-rated " ONE BATTLE AFTER ANOTHER " which was neither Feast or Famine and devoid of ... basically anything other than extreme over acting and little idea what its ambiguous story was meant to be about . This , however is a deeply considered movie set in the past in Brazil.which was emerging from the type of violation of democracy that the USA is sinking into . A better movie all round.
author: alanlamont-34154

title: Admire its novel approach, interesting pacing, and colourful characters, but it lacks tension and purpose.
text: If you are the type of person who sees a title like "The Secret Agent," and need that film to be a high intensity, tense spy thriller that has you at the edge of your seat...you probably will not like this film, I'll be honest with you.The Secret Agent is a surprisingly laissez-faire film; breezy and almost documentarian in pace, more of a slice of life film than anything else. On some level, I found that very refreshing. It's not the isolated, cold, "competence porn" audiences typically expect. Perhaps appropriately (being Brazilian), it's a lot more laid back and feels like a social drama, involving many characters of varying importance, who come in and out of our protagonist's life.The eponymous "secret agent," in this film isn't a reference to an actual secret agent, rather the sort of secretive, spy-like lives people are forced to live in an authoritarian system. When opposition becomes a crime, in effect, everyone becomes a secret agent.I really enjoyed the committment to the time period, the sets and the costumes, the performances in isolation are really great. The film plays around with the editing in ways that are both fun and fresh, and at other times, makes the narrative somewhat confusing.I love Wagner Moura in pretty much everything he's in, although - and I appreciate this may be because I don't speak Portuguese - I didn't find him *that* impressive.While I admire the film for taking such an interesting approach, I did find myself quite disconnected from the narrative for long stretches of the film. The characters and conflicts were just not interesting enough for me and I felt legitimately confused as to what the cause of the conflict was. The film does eventually lead to emotional intensity, but it came too late for me, personally.While I enjoyed The Secret Agent, it didn't blow me away and it's honestly not the best film about this time period I've seen.
author: ryanpersaud-59415

title: The lost side of humanity
text: "The Secret Agent" is Brazil's latest nomination for the Oscar's International Feature category. Filled with Brazilian northeastern 'ginga', the somewhat weird film has been claiming important awards ever since its 2025 Cannes debut. The main plot follows Marcelo, an on-the-run former teacher and tech expert that flies back to Recife - PE, where he will soon find out he is still in danger.Wagner Moura's portrayal is amazing and deserving of the worldwide acclaim he is receiving (even earned the 2026 Golden Globe for Best Actor). Filled with many non-actors, the splendid casting is also nominated for this new Academy Award category, and I must mention some of my favorite depictions: Dona Sebastiana, deputy Euclides and the gas station attendant. The cinematography and the production design deserve reverence as well. A perfect recreation of the city of Recife circa 1977, praised as accurate and very pretty to watch.The story mixes reality with local folklore references and even Roman mythology (Janus cat) whenever possible. Most of what is seen has no explanation or motive and the audience is left with many loose ends. Nonetheless, a few themes are constantly present, some with repetition. Did you notice death, blood, shark throughout the film? And the lack of explanation, would that be like real life, where so much happens without a noticeable reason or aim?It seems the director Kleber Mendonça Filho wanted to make the audience think about what was left unsaid, while showing us the cheap side of humanity, selling itself for money, corruption and power on all levels of society. Not much has changed since 1977, or ever mankind came to exist.
author: lyoneduardo

title: excellent acting common script
text: Wagner Moura, as always, embodies his characters and deserves to win an Oscar. The film's plot highlights a very dark period in Brazilian history without providing a direct historical deep dive. It portrays scars that we still carry today, such as corruption, the malfunctioning of the public administration, and impunity. Unlike I'm Still Here, which directly delves into the subject and the government's direct persecution, this film shows that there is no way to confront those who have the machinery of the state on their side.
author: JooP-8237

title: Fever dream (pejorative)
text: I don't know what I'd need to know about Brazil's military dictatorship period in order to grasp the purpose of this movie, if that would be helpful at all; clearly the director believes a point is being made here based on the camera constantly lingering on portraits of the president like it's a student film (something like Leviathan, a paragon of subtlety in comparison, merely includes a portrait in the shot), but judging the movie as a movie, not a visual companion to history textbooks or a vibe-focused autobiography, I thought this was atrocious.Being chauffeured around on this tour of a cornucopia of colorful Brazilian characters, all set in decently atmospheric locations, is entertaining enough for a while - that while, for me, lasted about 30 minutes, thereat I started to really wonder when the plot would begin in earnest. The answer is it takes the Secret Agent until almost half of its outrageous 161 minute runtime to realize it forgot to begin the story, so Wagner Moura and some other characters, introduced for that scene, sits down on a couch to explain to the audience what's going on. While editing is utilized to make this scene have a bit more life to it, that didn't prevent it from cementing the movie in my mind as one filled with almost Netflix-like exposition, which, to be entirely fair, is appropriate here since you would expect the viewer to be asleep or on their phone by that point; if you aren't however, it's simply jarring to hear, for example, someone explain their family tree to a character who knows it (on account of being part of it) in this awards darling.And speaking of awards, of the four Oscar nominations it definitely deserves to win best casting, you're immediately impressed by the charisma of all the various guys they've got in this thing, however since there's very little connection between and rarely any point to the endless rambling vignettes they populate, they don't contribute to how enjoyable the movie is. It's surprising the cinematography didn't get a nod - I'm not saying this because it was that great, but because they used an awful lot of lenses in ways that were meant to draw attention to that fact, and Academy voters are famous for voting for whatever's easy to notice. Wagner Moura being nominated for best acting is absolutely baffling to me, he doesn't have a wide variety of expressions in this, and if there are subtleties to his performance, he must've acted so hard they ended up being imperceptible, though based on how he visibly struggles to stay awake in some moments (which - same, buddy), I doubt that's the case. My best guess would be this is a proxy nod/award for Narcos, and I would not recommend watching the Secret Agent on that basis.
author: CamaradeThia

title: Engaging Theme with Uneven Performances
text: The Secret Agent is an engaging and visually appealing film that explores corruption and how powerful individuals can damage their own countries for personal gain. Its message about moral decay and abuse of power feels relevant and thought-provoking, giving the story meaningful weight. The atmosphere is enhanced by a beautiful and well-chosen musical score that complements the film's tone and emotional moments.However, while the concept and direction are strong, some performances fall short of their potential. Not all actors deliver the same level of intensity or depth, which slightly weakens the overall impact. Despite these flaws, The Secret Agent remains a solid and worthwhile watch, driven by its themes, mood, and striking presentation.
author: sami2005

title: The king is naked
text: The king is naked but everybody's saying that his clothes are the bomb, so you go with the flow. People are misunderstandig lack of good storytelling and cinema technic with "art".I love Kurosawa, Koreeda, Sofia Coppola, no problem at all with "slow" or "dense" movies.As a brazilian speaker the acting is laughable. author: renatolgtr

title: Let Brazil Cook
text: Honestly, this movie was better than I expected. Last year, we had "I'm Still Here", and now, Brazil just cooked another masterpiece.The Secret Agent is a really good movie, I just expected something else from the title, but even so, I really liked how it turned out.There were some jokes here and there that made me laugh, and the story is pretty solid for the most part, and sometimes, I found myself nervous, not knowing what would happen next.At times, the movie stops to focus on the main plot and just shows the characters bonding and having fun, like the whole horror leg plot, which was unexpected, but a good bit.The ending, on the other hand, was a bit underwhelming, it does make sense and it was foreshadowing towards it, but I did expected more.But in general, it's a really good movie, and I hope it wins "Best International Film".
author: NotMaya

title: What a masterpiece!
text: I've lasted sometime to watch it, mainly because I do not believe in hype, and I have to say what a movie! So many layers, sharp cast, a non obvious script, outstanding direction, a great pleasure, I must say. So sensible theme treated with intelligence and subtlety. The dark power of political and economic relationships, this incestuous dynamic between public and private world portraited so smart. A necessary work to keep us alert against raising of totalitarianism, abuse and economic interests on the opposite of individual freedom and the tragedy of manipulation. And do not forget to mention racism encapsulated along the movie.
author: juvenalmarcelosantos

title: Between Legs and Sharks.
text: Brazilian director Kleber Mendonça Filho writes and directs this thriller that reflects the dictatorship in Brazil. A period drama that evolves into a gripping historical portrait of a dark period in Brazilian society.Starring an impeccable Wagner Moura, the film is set in 1977, during Brazil's military dictatorship. Marcelo, a teacher trying to escape a turbulent past, returns to the city of Recife hoping for a fresh start and to reunite with his son. However, he soon discovers that the city is far from the refuge he imagined, as government forces are hunting him and death threats hang over him.The accolades are more than well-deserved for *O Agente Secreto*, a powerful film that, beneath the urban legend, hides a human thriller about daily life in Brazil during the dictatorship. It's a story about a community that offers a narratively complex yet highly engaging tale. The film navigates genres with ease, resulting in an explosive and delightful mix of drama, crime, thriller, and even giallo, with well-placed doses of humor.It's a thriller that needs time to reveal its true nature. Between the darkness of its characters and the folly of their surroundings, the film conceals an intense protagonist with whom you gradually become involved, feeling like you're part of this entire survival journey. It goes far beyond what its title suggests and offers different interpretations, not only of what happened at that specific moment, but also before and after.A vigorous and genuine exercise in pure genre, inspired and captivating. Few filmmakers today manage to transport us to a time, a place, and stories that ignite fascination, where each character conceals an intriguing plot waiting to be uncovered. It overflows with a contagious, anarchic energy while portraying the rot of the dictatorship and conveying a profound love for Brazil, its history, its customs, and, of course, for cinema itself. Kleber Mendonça Filho stands out for his intelligence in every sense and surrounds himself with a cast that gives their all on screen, ensuring that we never feel the film is forced, but rather that it's a story that envelops us like the most gripping intrigue before our eyes.It's a charming film that doesn't strive for perfection as its greatest achievement, but rather finds in its humanity a way to transform its flaws into great virtues. Its uniqueness accompanies a hypnotic narrative that moves between the passage of time and the present day. It's a work that doesn't hide its message and dares to offer its own version of a part of Brazilian history, a proposition that proves more than satisfactory.
author: saolivaresm

title: A Film That Doesn't Give Closure
text: Before watching this movie, I honestly think it helps to read a bit about Brazil's history. Nothing deep, just basics. That helped me a lot. Otherwise, the movie can feel confusing. It's non-linear and slightly complicated, especially if you're not familiar with Brazil's political background.The film is slow-paced, but it's full of small details. Every scene feels loaded. It talks about corruption, colonial hangover, trauma, power, and societal decay of that period, not directly, but always in the background.This is not a movie that ends when the credits roll. Some films are easy, you watch, you understand, finished. But this is the kind of film that stays in your head. What you understand in the first watch is actually very little. When you think about it again and again, more meanings start opening up. A rewatch will definitely reveal a lot of symbolism and hidden details.The ending might feel frustrating at first because there is no proper closure. You'll even question the director. But that's the point. The real incidents this film reflects never got closure, and that unresolved trauma is exactly what the film makes us feel. Once that clicks, the ending makes sense.Yes, at times it feels laggy, but the mystery keeps you going. In the third act, once violence enters, the film becomes more intense and gripping.The movie is completely about corruption, internal, external, political, societal, everywhere. Even small dialogues and background details are filled with it. That's when you realise this is not just Brazil's story. Corruption exists everywhere.It also shows a harsh truth: justice is expensive. Justice mostly belongs to those who can afford it.Because of all these layers and detailing, the Oscar nomination makes complete sense. This is not simple cinema, it's cinema that asks you to think.Overall, a good watch. Not for everyone, but if it works for you, it really stays with you.
author: Abyjith10

title: Peak actors, terrible writing
text: I've been watching Brazilian entertainment since the 90s. In many cases, the acting in Brazil is superior than in Hollywood, but lately the writing is getting worse and worse.Wagner Moura is a great actor, he was the best in the Tropa de Elite movies and also in the Escobar Netflix show, for example. He's also quite good in this movie The Secret Agent, but unfortunately the movie is just not that good.The Secret Agent ends up being a 4/10 movie, longer than it needed to be, very slow pace and with an enormous amount of political and social propaganda, to the point that you'll be rolling your eyes every 15 minutes.You know the writers messed up when you end up rooting for the bad guys. That's what happened with this movie. The good guys are obnoxious and full of themselves and the bad guys are actually more genuine and, ultimately, funnier.Hopefully Brazilian entertainment can go back to the good old days of Cidade de Deus.Thank you for your attention.
author: Hazu29

title: Secretly dismal.
text: The Secret Agent is premised on a professor ( Moura) in hiding due to reasons that surface later in the film. This gives the director plenty of scope to flesh out the day to day corruption, secrecy, disregard of life, and general living conditions of the 1970s military rule of Brazil. This is done with excellent cinematography and a plethora of characters and dialogue. Where the film fails is its inability to hook the audience into being invested or caring. A lot of the scenes were mundane , lacking coherency , intensity and engrossment. It masquerades as a thriller but lacks thrills- it's more a social commentary of the time. Perhaps a better understanding of Brazilian life and subculture would have helped but the director is appealing to an international audience so no excuse here.( many foreign films have an unfamiliar culture portrayed to the audience much more successfully ). The acting is good, and it feels very realistic thanks to the cinematography. A reasonable third act with a fair resolution cannot save this disappointment. Give it a miss. It will not win best film despite its surprise nomination.
author: deleyshaun

title: Everyman in a political nightmare
text:
If you're not fond of the sight of blood, you might think twice about seeing "The Secret Agent."
Blood is the glue trying together the chapters of this Brazilian political thriller just nominated for four Academy Awards after winning the best-actor Golden Globe for Wagner Moura.Blood is both symbol and metaphor ... not to mention literal, as gallons of the stuff flows through the fast-paced action. It comes from numerous donors, including one gnarly shark. Sharks, and the movie "Jaws," are another recurrent theme for writer-director Kleber Mendonca Filho.Marcelo Alves (Moura) is not a secret agent. Instead, he is a one-time university professor
forced underground by Brazil's military dictatorship of the 1970s after defying a ruthless industrialist trying to appropriate one of his patents.Now Marcelo is living in limbo in the city of Recife at carnaval time, hiding under an assumed identity in a halfway house for leftist enemies of the state, awaiting forged documents so he and his young son can flea the country.So rather than a badge of James Bond-style prowess, "Secret Agent" is, instead, a label bestowed on him by the authoritarian regime. A label, like illegal alien. Or domestic terrorist. Or a very nasty person ...
Filmmaker Filho paints the screen with washed-out primary colors and other details of '70s culture. Marcelo (that's no longer his name) drives a rickety yellow VW bug through streets teeming with revelers in front of gaudy storefronts. The line blurs between mardi gras hedonism and the frightened vigilance and other sorts of paranoia pervading life 24/7.Bullying oppression goes hand-in-hand with wall-to-wall corruption. The hired hitmen on Marcelo's trail are in cahoots with - or no different from - the father-and-sons goons running the police force. The journalists entrusted with reporting "the news" have to resort to coded urban legends like "the hairy leg," a disembodied appendage that attacks LGBT+ lovers in public parks, rather than reporting the actual police brutality that leaves its victims in hospitals and morgues.Moura's performance spans the broadest range of emotions, from loving father and husband to an Everyman caught in a Kafkaesque political nightmare. Writer-director Filho has also received his share of awards beginning at Cannes, not just for the detail of this period piece, but for viscerally sharing the pit-of-the-stomach terror of living under an authoritarian dictatorship.Audiences elsewhere in the world, like those in his native Brazil, are familiar with the feeling.In the U. S., it's still a new sensation.
author: rickchatenever

title: Very Boring Movie
text: As usual, these "weird" movies find their way to the Oscars 😂. The Night Agent has a painfully slow pace, and for long stretches you feel like you're watching clues without a map. The atmosphere and production are solid, and there are moments that look genuinely well made, but the script feels messy and underwritten. Instead of building tension, it drifts, repeats itself, and leaves key beats unclear-so the payoff doesn't hit the way it should. With a tighter edit and a clearer narrative spine, this could've been a really strong film. As it is, it's more frustrating than intriguing.
author: josetron

title: Politically motivated garbage
text: This is a movie that has been created to push a single political agenda, as should be obvious. Communists good, capitalists bad. The cinematography is great, but the acting is stale and the pacing kills any hope of excitement. This is amateurish work. Stylistically it seeks only to copy the work of better filmmakers. The story itself is childishly simple; modern cinematography is used to draw it out and make it look like something more. There is nothing to see here.
author: maxmill-21060

title: Insightful!
text: I had a great time with this movie, it gave me a great insight about Brazil during that period that I find really interesting. Somehow it's keeps you hooked to the trama. The acting was great. I understand now why is nominated, it is really good. Waner has become one of my favorite actors, he is very versatile, carries the whole movie on his shoulders, which I find outstanding.
author: CarlosMCabrero

title: Overhyped
text: Not the film I expected especially after the Oscar nominations. Went to the theatre today buzzing, what I walked into though was rough pacing, mid soundtrack and story lacking. 5 at best. I just couldn't connect to it and I really tried. There are much better films to consume even in 2025 alone. Disappointed.
author: fryzeegunner-83624

title: Masterclass
text: This film is superb. I would recommend reading about it before watching, as being able to recognize the complex political context, brilliant nods to Brazilian folklore, etc will make the viewing more enjoyable and easier to understand scenes that may come across random or unnecessary that contribute to a deep story drenched in the complicated and impactful reality of Brazil's history and present times. The cinematography, humour, soundtrack and bright colours of costumes and set bring the same level of complexity as the politics and violence to the visual front.
author: RaidonA

title: Slow, hard to follow and too long, The Secret Agent is redeemed by a solid 3rd act
text: I headed into this really looking forward to it but was quickly met by the realisation that this was not well catered to the uninitiated, given that without any knowledge of 1970s Brazilian politics, I was missing out on key plot details and character motivations. The failure to convey necessary context is a valid criticism, considering that the film is catering to an international audience. The plot moves glacially for the first two hours, and despite being stylishly shot, I did not find it engaging and was prepared to give an more negative review. However, the final act is more tense, action-packed and intriguing thematically, so it ended on a strong point.
author: Stargrazingreviews

title: Much Ado About Nothing
text: The Brazilian film industry is living through an almost unprecedented moment of recognition, both at home and abroad. For years, the idea persisted that Brazil did not know how to make cinema, as if our production were limited to empty comedies from Rede Globo. Today, we are finally beginning to look at our own cinema with more maturity. The problem starts when recognition automatically becomes a synonym for quality. That is exactly where The Secret Agent enters the discussion.I came in with high expectations. I did not watch it in a movie theater, but the weight of Cannes, the Critics Choice Awards, and the Golden Globes had already created a promise of greatness. The shock was immediate: international praise does not guarantee personal involvement. What is acclaimed does not always connect with the viewer.I love historical productions, especially when major periods are shown through the eyes of those who are not at the center of power. That was how I saw The Secret Agent: the chance to follow an ordinary civilian being crushed by the dictatorship. Something very different from I'm Still Here (2024), which follows Rubens Paiva, a politician, a man from the social and intellectual elite. Here, the promise was different. The problem is that it is never fulfilled.The main obstacle is the screenplay: bloated, exhausting, and without a clear direction. There are too many unnecessary scenes and a constant sense of dispersion. It takes two hours and forty minutes for the film to finally try, only at the end, to say clearly what it actually wants to be.The much-talked-about "hairy leg" is the symbol of this detour. The Northeastern legend appears in a scene that is completely out of place, extremely stupid, and dramatically useless. It deepens nothing, expands no theme, and contributes nothing to the narrative. It only interrupts the rhythm and provokes the inevitable question: what am I watching?If it lost an hour of runtime, went through a new edit, and focused more on Marcelo's escape and his motivations, the film would be infinitely better. It lacks synthesis and suffers from indulgence.Still, there are clear merits. Wagner Moura delivers an impeccable performance. Tânia Maria is the great revelation, with a magnetic screen presence. The recreation of the 1970s is excellent: costumes, sets, cinematography, and aesthetic references are all carefully crafted. Technically, it is a rich and meticulous work.But cinema, above all, is screenplay. And this screenplay is fragile. When it fails, everything else becomes ornament. It can be beautiful, sophisticated, and well-produced, but it is never enough to compensate for the lack of narrative strength.I watched it on the eve of the Oscar nominations and, like any good Brazilian, I still hope it gets nominated. International recognition carries symbolic weight. It is just tiring to pretend that awards are synonymous with quality. They are, first and foremost, politics.The Secret Agent rides on the impact created by Walter Salles's film. Both deal with the dictatorship, but the similarities end there. While one is striking and revolutionary, the other is diffuse and easily forgettable. One establishes itself as a reference work. The other sounds like a footnote.It hurts to write this, because I wanted to like it. But The Secret Agent does not seem strong enough to survive the test of time in the popular imagination.
author: LuanMarques1992

title: Interesting, but weak as an experience.
text: Kleber Mendonça Filho has succeeded in maturing his filmmaking, and uses a more sophisticated language in "The Secret Agent" than in his previous films; the opening scene makes it clear that it will be a film that will work with subtext at various points. Despite having a well-developed subtext, "The Secret Agent" often feels like a film with a drawn-out script; its long duration is unnecessary, and this is evident in many moments. I don't know why many critics consider "The Secret Agent" a thrilling film because, for the most part, the film is slow, and not a meditative or poetic slowness, but a monotonous, everyday slowness... This may also be connected to the editing work, which could have made the film more dynamic. Despite these problems, "The Secret Agent" is a very interesting story about forgotten people, about crimes that are never punished, and of course, about Brazil. It's important to remember that Kleber Mendonça Filho is a person who carries a very politicized vision in his films, and this makes them more superficial than profound. There is no great philosophical work, no reflection that truly transcends pragmatism; this is one of Kleber Mendonça Filho's biggest flaws-being a filmmaker who looks at many things askance and forgets that the beauty of cinema lies in its richness philosophical, on the human side, in aesthetic refinement, etc...
author: metodico

title: Excess of Metaphor, Scarcity of Meaning
text: This review is written by a Brazilian cinephile. The fact that this is a national production does not impose on me any obligation to offer automatic praise, nor does it lead me to overlook flaws in the name of cultural patriotism. For me, cinema is, above all, precision, coherence, and narrative rigor. Having watched nearly seven thousand films over several decades - not counting the many titles revisited multiple times - I feel fully justified in offering a frank and uncompromising analysis, regardless of a film's country of origin. MarceloThe Secret AgentMarcelo (2025) was my first encounter with the work of Kleber Mendonça Filho, and unfortunately it resulted in deep disappointment.The film presents itself as a political thriller set in 1977, during Brazil's military dictatorship, but stumbles precisely where it should be most solid: narrative clarity, internal coherence, and dramatic discipline. From the opening moments, there is a tendency toward redundant symbolism. The decision to show full-screen photographs of artists who were prominent in Brazil in 1977 clearly aims to anchor the story in its historical period. However, only moments later, a title card explicitly states that the story takes place in 1977. The effect is unnecessary and weakens the introduction, as if the film felt compelled to overemphasize something already established.The gas station sequence exemplifies another recurring issue: the sacrifice of plausibility in favor of blunt allegory. Armando arrives at the station and there is a corpse abandoned beside it, exposed in the open air. Even after two policemen arrive, the body is ignored; one of them prefers to question Armando and search his car. Even considering the authoritarian context of the era, this situation is unacceptable from a basic standpoint of realism: a decomposing body would produce a strong odor, pose sanitary risks, and require immediate removal. Everything suggests that the corpse was deliberately left there as a symbolic representation of death made banal under the dictatorship. If that was the intention, the result is misguided and excessive, as it breaks the internal logic of the scene and undermines credibility.During the opening credits, the film briefly cuts away from Wagner Moura inside the car to return to the image of the corpse lying on the dirt. This repetition adds no new meaning, deepens no metaphor, and advances nothing narratively. It is merely another visual underline that reinforces the impression of empty insistence.The screenplay's structure weakens further with the abrupt introduction, around the 43-minute mark, of two young women sitting in front of laptops, listening to cassette tapes labeled with the year 1977 and the name Marcelo. The scene interrupts the main narrative without any explanation. We do not know who these women are, what time period they belong to, or how they relate to Armando. Only at the end of the film, when one of them speaks with a doctor - Armando's son - does it become clear that they are students researching the dictatorship era and the events surrounding Armando. This information, deliberately withheld, does not generate productive mystery; it merely causes confusion. A brief contextualization at the beginning would have sufficed. As presented, the scene feels intrusive and poorly integrated.Another example of narrative excess is the scene involving the German tailor Hans, played by Udo Kier. The character adds nothing to the plot, clarifies no relationships, and advances no conflict. It is a sterile detour that merely extends the runtime. The impression is that of a personal concession by the director to the actor, who passed away in November 2025, but cinema cannot be sustained by implicit tributes. Notably, Armando himself seems to share the viewer's boredom, leaving the scene without even acknowledging the delegate. Several similar moments appear throughout the film, scenes that exist without clear dramatic function.To be fair, the period reconstruction is impeccable. In this respect, the film deserves genuine praise. Cars, costumes, hairstyles, portraits of President Geisel on the walls, and the films playing in theaters in 1977 all demonstrate meticulous research and attention to detail. Unfortunately, this visual rigor contrasts sharply with a basic problem in the dialogue: imprecise diction. At several points, lines are difficult to understand, which further compromises an already confusing narrative.Information gaps multiply. In the secret meeting between Armando and Elza and Valdemar inside a movie theater, nothing is explained. Who are these people? What is their concrete relationship to Armando? Why are they recording the conversation? The film offers no sufficient clues for the viewer to construct meaning, treating the scene as if its importance were self-evident.The most problematic - and frankly embarrassing - moment occurs around the 1h49 mark, in the infamous leg sequence. Grandiose music accompanies the image of a severed leg lying on a lawn. This is followed by nighttime scenes in a public square where several people engage in sex as if it were an improvised outdoor brothel. The same leg appears walking among them. A cut shows a woman reading a newspaper article about the incident, oddly describing it as a "shadow" rather than a leg. Returning to the square, the leg begins attacking people. What had been, however flawed, a political drama suddenly veers into grotesque horror. The sequence is absurd, tonally incoherent, and ridiculous. Later we learn it is the same leg found inside a shark and stolen from the morgue by a corrupt policeman, who throws it into a river. If the goal was to make it disappear, throwing it into a river where it could float and be found again is an elementary contradiction. The film ends without ever clarifying the leg's narrative or symbolic relevance.The musical score and incidental music further aggravate the experience. They are intrusive, excessively dramatic, and emotionally manipulative, often at odds with the images. Rather than sustaining tension, they generate fatigue and irritation.Logical inconsistencies pile up in the final act. When Armando asks the delegate for help, he manages to identify Vilmar, the hired killer. Yet Marcelo points him out to the police while the man is shown from behind, talking on the phone, even though moments earlier Marcelo had not seen his face and had only been called by name. Soon after, Vilmar phones Augusto Borba, who answers at the beach near a public phone. How did the assassin know where to find him? After killing two policemen, Vilmar escapes and passes right by the officer guarding the entrance gate, who inexplicably lets him go.In the ensuing chase, Abdias, Borba's partner, pursues Vilmar. A seated bystander, upon noticing a bloodstain on the ground, spontaneously tells Abdias which direction the killer went. How did he know Abdias was chasing the murderer? After Abdias is killed, a newspaper reports that he was shot twice in the face, while the film clearly shows a single shot from behind. The same newspaper also gives the delegate's name as Leonardo Ferreira Monteiro, even though throughout the film he is identified as Euclides Cavalcanti. These are gross errors, difficult to excuse.Even the film's title is misleading. MarceloThe Secret AgentMarcelo suggests that Armando is some sort of clandestine operative, when in fact he is an ordinary citizen. One cannot expect viewers to know that during the dictatorship the term was informally used to describe unidentified agents linked to the regime. That context should be made clear within the film itself.Unanswered questions abound: Who is João Pedro, who appears to be Armando's superior? Who is the businessman Henrique Ghirotti? Why does he hire Augusto Borba and Abdias to kill Armando, when it is Armando who seems to harbor deep hatred toward Ghirotti? The logic behind the central motivations remains obscure.MarceloThe Secret AgentMarcelo ends as it begins: laden with symbolic pretensions but lacking narrative cohesion, dramatic clarity, and internal rigor. What could have been a sharp portrait of a dark chapter in Brazilian history dissolves into a collection of poorly articulated scenes, exaggerated allegories, and basic screenplay errors. The final impression is not one of productive unease, but of perplexity and exhaustion. A film that demands much from its audience, yet offers very little in return.
author: Hildebrando_Martins_Almeida

title: Rather than focusing on large-scale espionage spectacle, the film emphasizes tension through character interaction.
text: The Secret Agent is one of Alfred Hitchcock's lesser-known spy thrillers, yet it offers an intriguing early look at the director's obsession with suspense, moral ambiguity, and the cost of deception. Loosely based on Joseph Conrad's novel, the film follows an undercover British agent whose mission blurs the line between duty and conscience.Rather than focusing on large-scale espionage spectacle, the film emphasizes tension through character interaction. John Gielgud's agent is not a traditional heroic figure; he is quiet, conflicted, and emotionally restrained. This choice makes the film feel introspective, highlighting how secrecy corrodes personal relationships. Madeleine Carroll's performance adds emotional weight, especially as trust begins to fracture under the pressure of lies.
author: goheji

title: Rather than focusing on large-scale espionage spectacle, the film emphasizes tension through character interaction.
text: Peter Lorre stands out as the most memorable presence. His unsettling charm and unpredictability inject the film with dark humor and menace, foreshadowing Hitchcock's later fascination with morally ambiguous side characters.Stylistically, The Secret Agent shows Hitchcock still refining his craft. Some pacing issues and tonal shifts-particularly the mix of comedy and tragedy-can feel uneven. However, the film's central idea is strong: espionage is not glamorous, but morally compromising.
author: yedeyi

title: And then all Hell breaks loose. For 5 minutes.
text: Created with painstaking detail while divulging its narrative twists sparingly, Kleber Mendonça Filho's The Secret Agent is a slow burn delight. By the two hour mark the audience is treated to multiple absorbing digressions and a number of delicious tracking shots.Yet the exact nature of the film's plot remains elusive. By the film's conclusion multiple dangling threads remain and our natural desire for closure stymied. This goes to the film's themes of history getting lost in the fragility of memory. The modern day postscript sees the younger generation reconstructing the past as an effort to avoid historical mistakes.The ominous and dreadful opening scene sets the tone for late 70s Brazil. The Policia Militar are more interested in harassing Wagner Moura's Marcelo (Armando) than they are in investigating a dead body in the road. The dead body can't pay a bribe.Moura established his reputation in America off the back of his towering depiction of Pablo Escobar in Narcos. It is a Television leading man performance which rivals the works of Gandolfini and Cranston. The actor offers another bravura performance in The Secret Agent, one that highlights his range. Where his Columbian drug lord is an angsty raging alpha, his research professor is an often taciturn open-hearted omega.The Secret Agent brings to mind more recent South American history. Perhaps the hammer isn't mightier than the pen.Sometimes only time will tell.
author: RyanM-350

title: Neither serves the thrill nor the politics!
text: Aside from the captivating opening sequence and a somewhat engaging climax, I find that the film falls short of achieving its intended objectives, whether as a political critique or a thrilling narrative.Instead, it seems to linger in an 'in-between' space that feels distant and elusive, making it hard for me to appreciate its qualities or maintain my interest throughout its duration, if truth be told, it just didn't hold my attention.Wagner Moura's portrayal of Armando is commendable; he infuses the character with warmth and authenticity.However, considering the backdrop of the story, i.e. Brazil under a military dictatorship in '77, I anticipated a far more impactful and profound exploration of the subject matter in hand.I can still vividly remember Walter Salles' 'I'm Still Here,' which had garnered accolades at the Academy Awards previously, which In my humble opinion, was a much superior film that not only made the core characters feel close to me but also intricately wove comparable themes about dictatorship, murders, societal anarchy etc.
author: SoumikBanerjee1996

title: the jaws of the past
text:
There is no espionage story in 'O agente secreto' ('The Secret Agent') by Brazilian director
Kleber Mendonça Filho, nor is there any connection to the novel written by James Conrad more than a century ago, which inspired many other films and television series. It is one of those films that is difficult to fit into a single cinematic style, which can belong to several genres or for which a special category should be invented. The film borrows its title from a scene in which the main character passes through a cinema hall in which the credits of a film (imaginary, with Belmondo!) of that name are being projected. 'O agente secreto' is also full of references to cinema around 1977 and in particular to Steven Spielberg's 1975 blockbuster 'Jaws' - and a screening room is the setting for several key scenes. This complex and interesting movie does not belong to the genre of films about films either. It combines political realism with Latin American fantastic to depict the struggle for survival of people living in a corrupt and violent society, in the midst of a period of dictatorship that lasted many decades and dominated the history of Brazil in the second half of the 20th century.The film starts as a road movie in the semi-desert landscape of northern Brazil, an arid country strewn with corpses and dominated by fear, to evolve into an atypical political thriller with a slow movie pace. The main hero is called Marcello or maybe Armando, as he sometimes hides his real identity. He flees from the authorities and the hired killers sent on his trail, waiting for the false passports that will allow him and his little boy to leave the country. The city of Recife where he arrives is a place of refuge and perhaps of transit to freedom or, in any case, to a world where his life is not in danger. A corner of a country dominated by political terror and endemic corruption. The reasons for his behavior will be gradually discovered by the viewers who will also get to know those around him: his deceased wife's parents, his neighbors - most of whom are fugitives like him -, the gullible politicians, the corrupt policemen and the hired killers, a kind of Tarantino-style heroes transported to Brazilian soil. Flashbacks will gradually complete the story of the film's hero's life, and the events that rush towards the end will only be clarified and put into context by an epilogue that takes place in our days.I also really liked the way the screenwriter and director Kleber Mendonça Filho built the narrative and how he brought it to the screen, transforming the cinematic spectacle into a violent realistic image of Brazil in the eighth decade of the last century. Mixed in are also elements of fantasy, as present in many novels and films of Latin America, which are packaged in this case with elements of Hollywood cinematic mythology. Spielberg and (again) Tarantino seem to have met in an episode of men under a history of dictatorship, fear and repression. I appreciated the remarkable gallery of portraits created by the filmmaker and exceptionally interpreted by a collection of formidable selected and guided actors. I will mention only two names: that of Wagner Moura who has already collected well-deserved awards for the lead role in the film and the German actor Udo Kier, in his last appearance on the screens in a heartbreaking supporting role. The full list should be very long. In my opinion, in this year with serious competition, 'O agente secreto' should collect several Academy Awards, and not just the one for Best Film in a Foreign Language.
author: dromasca

title: A well-recreated period
text: A perfect 70s aesthetic, evident in the costumes, hairstyles, and sets, as well as the film grain and camera angles. The lengthy setup of the plot requires some patience, but the script's reward is well worth the effort. Wagner Moura is indeed excellent. A beautiful way to conclude the film. To be savored with patience.
author: alain_pinel

title: Boring
text: While the espionage genre usually promises high-stakes tension and pulse-pounding intrigue, The Secret Agent delivers all the excitement of watching a committee file paperwork. Instead of a sharp, cinematic thriller, audiences are served a sluggish, heavy-handed narrative that feels less like art and more like a taxpayer-funded lecture.
author: vhpcampos

title: A terrific Wagner Moura
text: If you liked him as Pablo Escobar in Narcos and in Civil War, you will love Wagner Moura in this film. Although the movie runs long (2:41) and the story can get a little confusing, the end result is more than satisfying. Directed and written by Brazilian Kleber Mendonça Filho, the film takes the craziness of 1977 Brazil under a dictatorship, a story around a man trying to change his identity, and a large and talented cast (see in particular Tânia Maria as Dona Sebastiana) to make a film that I enjoyed from beginning to end.Moura plays a high-tech expert who is trying to evade his past by changing his name from Marcelo to Armando and moving to Recife during Carnival. His wife has died and his son Fernando lives with the maternal grandparents. Armando lives in a house run by Dona Sebastiana with other political refugees. He is trying to escape his past by taking a position in a local government records office. In a subplot that is not fully explained, he spends some of his time in the records office searching for lost identity records of his mother. Unfortunately, he cannot escape his past and he is targeted for assassination by a corrupt business executive who has a grudge against Marcelo/Armando. He is urged to flee the country while violence erupts among the local police and the hit men. The film ends with an update: Fernando is now a doctor in Recife but does not remember his father despite the availability of audio recordings and newspaper accounts.The above plot does not adequately describe Filho's wonderful storytelling that kept me spellbound for the entire movie. There is Moura's acting, the great cast full of colorful people, the setting of 1970s Brazil, and humor largely based around a shark (I won't give it away). This is definitely worth seeing.
author: ProfWinerReviews

title: A World Built on Suspicion
text: This one takes its time and doesn't rush anything. It's very talky, very slow, but in a way that feels intentional. The tension isn't about big moments, it's more about the constant unease, like something bad is always just around the corner.The mood does a lot of the work here. Everything feels heavy, suspicious, and morally messy, and that's what makes it interesting. On top of that, the art direction is excellent, with a strong visual identity and carefully composed frames that give the film a distinct look and atmosphere.Quiet, smart, and surprisingly gripping once it clicks.
author: augustovlucca

title: Pamphlet
text: Pamphlet from the Brazilian left that dominates state-funded cinema. The film is sold as a neo-noir thriller with Wagner Moura on the run from a mysterious past in Recife, but in reality it's a selective ode to the "victim" of the dictatorship: the pure, always-right, heroic leftist intellectual.Wagner Moura acts well (he always does), the cinematography lovingly captures 1970s Recife, there's decent pacing in parts and cool references (like Tubarão, the hairy leg). But the script is so manichean and one-sided that it becomes cheap propaganda. It only shows the side that suits today's PT narrative: "we were the pure oppressed good guys, the coup came from eternal evil." It deliberately ignores that corruption and authoritarianism don't have just one side - and that plenty of people on the left back then had their own skeletons.
author: joaopauloxrocha

title: Expected more, but good.
text: I had not even heard of The Secret Agent. I hadn't even seen a trailer and had to go to the ONE theater that was playing this film 50 miles away! But I was intrigued by the buzz around this film. Being a Brazilian film that was directed by Kleber Mendonça Filho I thought it was going to be great. Last year's great Brazilian film I'm Still Here which was one of the best films I saw made me go into this film with very high expectations.I have to say that even though I liked the film I was disappointed because I thought I would see a much better film. It is set in Brazil in 1977 during the Brazilian military dictatorship. Armando, played by Wagner Moura is a former professor who is fleeing persecution from the regime. He arrives in Recife to gets an apartment from a woman who gives shelter to various political refugees. Armando tries to reconnect with his son which had been raised by his in laws since his wife had died. Armando is in hiding because he's in danger and has to leave Brazil. All of this would be a lot more intriguing if the film was more focused. It is technically a thriller but director Kleber Mendonça Filho makes this film more about a collection of weird and quirky characters and a more satirical tone at times that reduce the tension. But the film feels more like a love letter to films the fire tor loves. There is a focus on Jaws, for instance that in many ways is a metaphor for what Armando is going through. Also, Sr. Alexandre, Armando's father in law is a projectionust at a Recife movie theater. There are so e great scenes there. Including huge line outside a showing of Jaws, and the audience reaction to The Omen. There's a plot point about a severed leg found in a shark that gets a lot of attention. Even a very funny dream sequence with that leg. Very funny scene but it breaks what should be a more compelling and tense story. When the 3rd act comes, there is some tension but not nearly as much as if it could have been there if the film has been more focused.Also, at almost 160 minutes the film is very slow.Good film, not as good as it could have beenGrade: B-
author: flicklover

title: Corruption, fear & quiet resistance-powerful masterpiece
text: Incredible movie set in 1977 Brazil dictatorship-corruption, police brutality, moral collapse everywhere (that shark leg scene is perfect satire). Armando on the run, mystery kept me hooked until reveals. Slow first half builds tension perfectly. Themes of resistance, fear, lost humanity hit hard. Wagner Moura amazing. Tiny issue: early slow burn, but worth it. Deserves all awards-perfect 10/10.
author: DrGadimov

title: Time doesn't care. *Spoilers*
text: The Secret Agent finds its greatest brilliance in the way it closes its own narrative. By revealing Armando/Marcelo's death through a newspaper, the film does something rare and profoundly human: it shows that life simply goes on. The son, who loved his father deeply, grows up - and because he lost him so early, barely remembers him as an adult. As happens with so many real lives.There is something harsh and deeply true in this. Our lives matter immensely while we are living them, yet many of them are lost in the vast sea of time and space. And when they do survive, they are not always remembered as they truly were. We do not control our own narrative - and the film seems painfully aware of this silent tragedy.That said, the film's pacing is debatable. The plot unfolds with great artistry and restraint, supported by exceptional production design. The faded tones of the 1970s are not merely aesthetic; they feel like a state of mind. The production is impeccable in every detail.Wagner Moura's performance is outstanding. At many moments, I completely forgot the actor and truly believed I was watching Marcelo/Armando himself. That kind of disappearance of the actor into the character is rare and powerful.By the end, I was deeply impressed - but I would not watch it again. This is not a film made for entertainment. It is intense, dense, almost emotionally exhausting. There is something cathartic about it, and perhaps even nihilistic, in the sense that it confronts the loss of meaning, the erosion of memory, and the indifference of time toward individual stories.The Secret Agent does not comfort. It confronts. And perhaps that is precisely why it leaves such a lasting impact.
author: lusobrosa

title: Ordinary corruption in beautiful colours
text: Brazil in the 1970s, military regime is brutal, and ultra-liberal economy feeds, as in other dictatorships, a rampant omnipresent corruption amongst the powerful, the uniformed and, basically, anyone who has information or a neighbour to sell.Kleber Mendonça Filho, the director and writer, has a taste for the resistance and resilience of ordinary people against institutionalised violence. His central characters, each of them brilliantly brought to the screen by an inspired cast, is un-ambiguous morally but ethically confused. From corrupt policemen and their lot to fleeing idealist and his network of helpers, they belong to a manichaeans world split, but their actions tend to get away from them and the side they incarnate. That prevents the 160 minutes length of the film to become boring.That, plus the beautiful cinematography. Colour becomes a language of itself, which often emphasises by opposition the darkness of the actions. For instance the drab repulsive corruption of day to day policemen is even more blatant when shot in a dried out extra sunny landscape of fields, by the whitewashed walls and roadsigns of a gas station, where a Yellow Volkswagen driver is their target feet away from a days old decaying body, which everybody ignores but a hoard of famished dogs.Beyond being a tense thriller, the movie is also a political work. Two movies are quoted, The Omen, and Jaws, two perfect caricatures of evil lurking below the surface of the United States colonial and eco-liberal politics of the sixties and seventies, often relying on authoritarian conservative regimes installation. Mendonça Filho may well be delivering us a warning as to the return of these times. Similarly, the suggestion that carnivals and/or sensationalist tabloid like medias participate in hiding from us deeper violence and oppression is probably not a gratuitous twist of the scenario. But this is done discreetly and does not distract from the core thriller genre of the movie: Individual running away from a corrupt network of powerful enemies and trying to save himself and his family.
author: jjr4

title: Intense and unsettling
text: The film has a very interesting and beautiful representation of Brazil in the 70s, full of cool references to the culture of the time, including films from the 70s that are now classic cults. The pacing builds up to a strong ending, that i have to admit i felt it was interrupted. The final persecution scene left everyone in the theater on the edge of their sits, but for some reason the director cuts for another scene and ends the film... but overall quite good film, it impresses me how brazilian cinema is evolving fast, one film better than the other, lately. The Secret Agent is not the action thriller in the "Hollywood" sense, because it has a lot of political undertones and works with cultural peculiarities from southern american countries that western cinema has dificulty to understand and value, but it's quite a unique film from a national industry that is learning how to make films using the aesthetics and comercial language from Hollywood, and it's working quite well.
author: felipearielm

title: Political Cinema Rooted in Myth, Streets, and Silence
text: What fascinated me most about The Secret Agent is how the film operates through silence, implication, and tension rather than exposition. Its political dimension is always present, but never didactic. Power, surveillance, fear, and masculinity are explored through gestures, absences, and atmosphere instead of speeches.
